## What is this post about?

This post is less technical and more of a little story on how the main mechanic for one of my university projects was found. The project in question is <a href ="https://portfolio.fh-salzburg.ac.at/projects/2024-the-wuzeling">The Wuseling</a>. Wuseling is a four player local  2.5D brawler about bearded mages that battle it out in an arena.

The game was written in Unity and my part in this project was mainly Gameplay-Programming and a bit in Game Design. At the start of the project we had to find our main mechanic so we created a few prototypes that I often ended up programming.

## Storytime! So what was our process?

In hindsight we actually somewhat followed the double/triple diamond design model.

### The Diamond Model

I first got into contact with this when I was reading <a href="https://www.amazon.com/Design-Everyday-Things-Revised-Expanded-ebook/dp/B00E257T6C">The Design of Everyday Things</a> by Don Norman (highly recommend that book by the way). 
<figure>
	<a href="/assets/img/devblog01/doubleDiamond.png"><img src="/assets/img/devblog01/doubleDiamond.png"></a>
	<figcaption style="text-align:center"><a href="/assets/img/devblog01/doubleDiamond.png" title="The double diamond design model">The double diamond design model</a>.</figcaption>
</figure>
Basically the design model describes the process of designing solutions for problems. Firstly one needs to define the problem itself. For this one broadens to find possible cuases for the problem to then converge back to find a concerte definition for the problem. Afterwards the same is done for finding a solution. Firstly many possible solutions are found to converge back to a concrete solution again.

Besides the two diamond model there also exsists the three diamonds model:
<figure>
	<a href="/assets/img/devblog01/tripleDiamond.webp"><img src="/assets/img/devblog01/tripleDiamond.webp"></a>
	<figcaption style="text-align:center"><a href="/assets/img/devblog01/tripleDiamond.webp" title="The triple diamond design model">The triple diamond design model</a>.</figcaption>
</figure>

This grafic is by Karl T. Ulrich who also has a lovely explanation on the triple diamond model <a href="https://ktulrich.com/the-triple-diamond-model-of-design/#:~:text=The%20triple%20diamond%20model%20focuses,establishing%20a%20product%20architecture%2C%20or">here</a>.
But in short it is quite similiar to the two diamond model. It however adds another diamond in the middle which focuses on understanding the needs of the customer/user.

**As a little sidenote:** personally I always tend to call it the tennisball-snake model (since it looks like a snake that has swallod a few tennisballs) :snake: :tennis:
Just now I was once again wondering why my google search for "tennisball-snake design model" didn't get me any good results :sweat_smile:
{: .notice}

don norman also describes the human centered design process in his book

it focuses on this and that


## Back to Wuseling!
Enough about the theory behind it!

now back on topic to wuseling



--> snake with tennisballs (we had the problem of never ending tho...) -> double diamond model of design (don norman) or (also vergleich triple diamond?)
double diamond: is from design thinking (vergleich don norman und design of everyday things book, can recommend https://www.amazon.com/Design-Everyday-Things-Revised-Expanded-ebook/dp/B00E257T6C). we had an altered version. original says, to first broaden then converge to first find the problem, then do the same for the solution. 
we didnt really have a problem in mind, it was a bit more open ended. we did define tho that it should be physics based, somewhat chaotic (hard to define btw, so kinda got lost later on) and include some pushing/pulling. after that was defined we jumped right into some broad brainstorming to find possible mechanics such as: *list em all

don norman also describes the human-centered design process
(grafik!)
think the image says it all. design is an iterative process of testing stuff and altering till it works. that we somehow also employed

tho i do get the feeling we had the problem of not finding an end to it. stuff never clicked 100%. In the end wuseling was scrapped and not continued. however if we had continued perhaps we would have ended with something that clicks in the end.

Cheers, thanks for the read ;)
